title: Zero-Shot Reward Specification via Grounded Natural Language

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## ZERO-SHOT REWARD SPECIFICATION VIA GROUNDED NATURAL LANGUAGE

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Rewards in reinforcement learning are difficult to design for most tasks and often require access to underlying state information. The alternatives to reward signals are usually demonstrations or goal images which can be labor-intensive to collect. Describing the goal as a text description is a low effort way of communicating the desired task to an agent. However, goal-text conditioned policies so far have been trained in scenarios either with rewards that assume access to states or with labeled expert demonstrations. In this work, we devise a model that leverages CLIP to ground objects in a scene described by the goal-text paired with spatial relationship rules to provide an off-the-shelf reward signal using only raw pixels as input. We distill the policies learned with this reward signal on several tasks to obtain a single text conditioned policy that can be applied to solve new tasks at deployment. We show the effectiveness of this framework on a set of robotic manipulation tasks.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Previous efforts have explored image-based goal specification, with significant successes in visual navigation and manipulation tasks (Pathak et al., 2018; Nair et al., 2018; Fu et al., 2018; Singh et al., 2019). Yet existing image-based goal specification paradigms are limited because they are typically limited to a particular scene _instance_ in an environment, whereas a _semantic_ goal comprises multiple possible scene configurations. Reinforcement learning offers one of the most appealing premises in the study of AI: from a reward signal alone, algorithms which learn optimal policies that maximize expected reward can learn to perform navigation, dexterous manipulation, and host of other impactful tasks. However, discovering or specifying a reward function for a given task is often a very challenging problem, especially when one is considering agents that can learn from uninstrumented environments, e.g., from raw image observations alone. We wish to have an agent that can learn purely from pixels, with no access to the underlying state of the environment at any point during learning or task execution. Achieving this goal without access to an instrumented reward function has been exceedingly challenging.

One can use image-based reward specification to cause a robot agent to navigate to a particular chair next to a specific tall plant, but that agent may not always succeed at the generic task of “go to a chair next to a tall flowering plant”: e.g., if the goal specification image shows a red chair next to

To be sure that the true goal is properly specified irrespective of the invariances of the model’s underlying perceptual representation, a user may have to provide a set of goal image examples that cover the variation of the target concept, potentially a very expensive undertaking to collect or generate.

We advocate _semantic reward specification via grounded natural language_ , where a user describes a goal configuration in the world using a natural language description referring to entities in the world. This direction has long been a “holy grail” of AI research and a presumed capability of AI science fiction—the ability to instruct a robot with natural language—yet attempts have been limited by the state of the art in grounded language perception. It also falls under the general umbrella of leveraging large-scale passive data to bootstrap embodied learning, where we rely on language as a means to provide necessary reward shaping missing in visual data alone.

Several previous efforts train reward functions or policies that take natural language as input for goal description (Oh et al., 2017; Bahdanau et al., 2018; Zhou & Small, 2020; Goyal et al., 2020; Fu et al., 2019; Hermann et al., 2017; Shao et al., 2020). They all however rely on reward signals that have access to state of the system or demonstrations of the task distribution they are training on. There are works that use human videos to learn reward functions to train their agent with (Shao et al., 2020; Sermanet et al., 2018; 2016), but they require a curated dataset of humans performing the tasks.

Recently however, the advent of large-scale multimodal training data together with large capacity language and vision deep learning models has significantly advanced the state of the art. A steady series of innovations have advanced grounded language modeling, from early work on multimodal translation and fusion models (Barnard et al., 2003; Quattoni et al., 2007; Guadarrama et al., 2016), to large-scale joint embedding models (Frome et al., 2013; Radford et al., 2021), to the plethora of multimodal transformer models currently under investigation (Su et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2019; Hu & Singh, 2021). CLIP, in particular, demonstrated a transformative advance on zero-shot object recognition (Radford et al., 2021).

We could specify a goal state to a robot by simply offering a description of the goal configuration in natural language and using the CLIP embedding dot product with an observed image to evaluate proximity to goal state. Surprisingly, this can work in simple cases, for examples see the top example in Figure 5. But for more complex goals performance can be poor, especially with complex captioned scenes involving spatial relationships and on domains that differ from web images (as shown in (Radford et al., 2021)) and the bottom example in that figure.

To overcome these limitations, we propose a factored grounding of ‘what’ vs ‘where/how’ aspects of goal state (Figure 2), and offer a novel spatial-salience scheme for the former. We argue that existing (e.g., CLIP-like) models can be used to ground the _what_ aspects of a goal quite effectively, including appropriate attribute and concept-level generalization, while a separate _where/how_ module can ground spatial relationship aspects of goal configuration. After training many tasks with this

reward scheme we learn a language conditioned policy that can execute new tasks in a zero-shot fashion – from a natural language description of task – without having to train a new policy for every new task.

In summary, our contributions are as follows:

1. A zero-shot reward function to learn a set of robotic manipulation tasks on raw pixels without access to state. (Zero-shot in the sense that no goal images or human demonstrations are provided at training time or test time.)
2. A distilled language-conditioned multi-task policy on several tasks learned using the zero-shot reward function which is able to deploy new unseen tasks.

## 2 METHOD

## 2.1 BASE ZERO-SHOT REWARD MODEL: DOT-PRODUCT VISUOLINGUISTIC REWARD

Our base model is the most intuitive way to use the CLIP model to compute reward. Our base model simply computes reward by taking a dot product between the goal language input feature and image observation feature through CLIP’s language and image encoders respectively: _rt_ = _EI_ ( _It_ ) _· EL_ ( _g_ ) A subset of tasks can be learned with this reward model. We visualize the limits of this model on two environments in Figure 5.

2.2 FULL ZERO-SHOT REWARD MODEL: SPATIALLY GROUNDED VISUOLINGUISTIC REWARD

**CLIP-Saliency for Phrase Grounding** We have developed a simple method which factors phrase grounding and spatial relationship processing, which can be used to specify a reward function that operates only on image content using a natural language description. Figure 2 illustrates our full model leverages CLIP’s encoders in a very different way than the base model. Our model first parses the goal language description _g_ into object noun phrases _go_ 1 _...gok_ and the desired object interaction. The object noun phrases are passed through CLIP’s language encoder and the current image observation is passed through CLIP’s image encoder. We use the language encodings as class embeddings of each object noun phrase to perform a saliency analysis (using Grad-CAM (Selvaraju et al., 2017)) on the image encoding of the observation on the last convolutional layer (specifically, the ReLU layer of CLIP’s ResNet-50 backbone). Saliency models such as Grad-CAM generally output a heatmap of the features that indicate a class exists in the current image input. The state extractor in Figure 2 computes the “state” of each object using the argmax of the saliency heatmap. See Figure 3.

**Spatial Grounding Heuristics** Our model determines task completion using a spatial grounding rule based reward function that takes the object pixel coordinates of one or more camera views and desired spatial relationship as input. Our spatial heuristics can be used to describe a broad set of manipulation tasks to push or place objects to semantic locations. A reward of 1 is given to the RL algorithm for every timestep that the conditions of the reward criteria are met for a given spatial relationship. The oracle reward uses true x,y,z spatial positions of the objects to determine if the desired spatial relationship

The first set (left of, right of, on top of, below, and in between) assume coordinates in a front camera view and the semantics are defined in that camera view with positive y in the upward direction and positive x in the right direction. The second camera is needed to know if the object is placed in the front or behind another object correctly. The third set (close to & inside of) require access to a front camera view with a 45 degree downward tilt towards the ground. This camera view is needed to see if the object is getting closer to another object in two orthogonal directions at once where as a left only or front view only allows you to determine one dimension of closeness. For “inside of” a 45 degree camera helps the agent see if the object is going inside another object without occlusion. The _ϵ_ threshold for “inside of” is much smaller than for “close to” since the centroid can come much closer when an object goes inside a container object.

## 2.3 LANGUAGE CONDITIONED MULTI-TASK POLICY

Now that we have a method for learning tasks in a zero-shot reward fashion, we would like to be able to not require training a new policy for every new task and ideally have a language conditioned multi-task policy that takes in the goal text description of an unseen task and executes the task without needing any new samples from the environment.

To approach this goal, we first learn several tasks via reinforcement learning using our zero-shot reward model. We then create a large dataset of the rollouts of those polices and pair each trajectory with the goal text description of the tasks it was trained to learn. We then use behavioral cloning (supervised learning for predicting actions) to learn a policy that takes images and text goal task description as input and actions as target outputs. We use CLIP’s language encoder as the text goal embedding that is fed to the multi-task policy. In addition we use image augmentation techniques to aid behavioral cloning in learning more robust policies.

## 3 EXPERIMENTS

## 3.1 PHRASE GROUNDING AND BASE ZERO-SHOT REWARD MODEL VISUALIZATION

Object detectors are one way to extract object states, however, they are usually not used off the shelf and need to be fine-tuned with in domain data. In Figure 3 we show pretrained Mask R-CNN (He et al., 2017) outputs on different camera views for the block stack environment. As you can see in the first two subfigures, the blocks are not proposed as objects with Mask R-CNN from both far and close camera views. This is a demonstration of the need for in-domain fine-tuning of object detectors to work in the environment you want to use. Our Grad-CAM output from CLIP however, highlights exactly the objects we are interested in from the object noun phrases that describes each object in the last two subfigures.

The CLIP model has been trained on a much larger dataset than Mask R-CNN and has a language component that allows us to request for the location of the object noun phrase of interest. With Mask R- CNN however, even if the object proposals were good, it would not necessarily classify objects it has not been trained for and therefore

In Figure 5 we show what our base reward model outputs on two goal descriptions: 1. inverted pendulum 2. yellow object close to a blue object. For the first goal description we observe that the dot product increases as the pendulum becomes more inverted from either side as desired. For the second goal description we observe that as the blue object gets closer to the yellow object the dot product increases except for the closest image where it dips which results in an undesired output. We observed through these two goal descriptions and many others that the base reward model is not sufficient for recognizing object spatial relationships. The encoders are good at identifying what objects are in the image however, which is what we leverage for our full reward model.

## 3.2 FULL ZERO-SHOT REWARD MODEL RESULTS

In Figure 6 we show how our full reward model performs on three manipulation tasks. We train each task using our full zero-shot reward model output as reward for the PPO reinforcement learning algorithm (Schulman et al., 2017). We then train with the oracle reward that has access to state and the base zero-shot reward model for comparison. Curiosity reward (Pathak et al., 2017; Burda

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

et al., 2018) is also used as our baseline since it only has access to images similar to our method for computing reward, but has only been successful for videogames such as Atari and Mario or Locomotion where exploring new states leads to progressing through the task (going further in levels of game for example). It is less privileged however, in that it does not use language input for task specification.

Curiosity learns the pushing pucks close together task but then starts learning separation of the pucks which reemphasizes that curiosity is only useful for tasks where exploring new dynamics leads to going farther in the task. Curiosity also has some trouble learning the double inverted pendulum task because the dynamics of the pendulum swinging can be hard to predict and therefore have misleading higher reward. Curiosity also does not learn the other manipulation tasks (stacking and placing) as those are more complex tasks that are harder to reach by exploration. For Double inverted pendulum (Fig. 4) our base reward model does better than oracle by chance which we speculate is because the oracle reward was originally designed for state input and was not tuned for learning image to reward mapping. Our base reward model fails for pushing, stacking, and placing, which take “an image of a yellow block on top of a red block”, “an image of a yellow object close to a blue object”, and “an image of a yellow block on the right of a red block” as language input for those tasks respectively. For pushing, stacking, and placing our full reward model performs almost as well as oracle sparse reward which has privileged access to state.

The oracle reward function for Double-Inverted-Pendulum is alive bonus minus distance penalty minus velocity penalty. The oracle for SawyerSimRobot-Pushing is a sparse reward that outputs one when the centroid distance between two pucks are below a threshold. The oracle reward for FetchSimRobot-Stacking is a sparse reward that outputs one when a yellow block is within a horizontal and vertical threshold distance of a red block. The oracle reward for FetchSimRobot-Placing is a sparse reward that outputs one when a yellow block is correctly placed on the right of a red block. See Figs. 3 & 5 for image observation examples of FetchSimRobot and SawyerSimRobot.

code as the conditioning input. The latter baseline simply labels the training tasks with integers 0 to 17. Since it only has primitive codes for the training tasks it uses CLIP’s language embedding to find the closest primitive code it was trained with to the test language goal description.

We use the same oracle reward that has direct access to state in figure 6 to measure the performance of our multi-task models. Every timestep the objects are in the correct target text description the agent is rewarded 1 point. The objects never start in the correct target state. Therefore it always takes several timesteps for the policy to move the objects to the correct state. All episodes timeout after 100 timesteps or if the one of the objects falls off the table. In RL training and in policy rollout collection for behavior cloning each object has a one block width of variation in starting point location. We tested on same distribution of start point variation interval (seen initial state distribution) and double the start point variation interval (unseen initial state distribution).

In Table 2 we see that no conditioning has the lowest performance as expected since there is full ambiguity in what task to perform given only an image. However, since the no conditioning model has been trained across many different object colors going to different target states it has learned general displacement of blocks to different semantic locations randomly that can sometimes be moved to the correct semantic location by chance during testing. For the training tasks it has higher performance than testing tasks since it can memorize to execute some of the training tasks that have almost the exact same initial states sampled during testing as those in the behavior cloning dataset and thereby execute correctly as memorized.

The primitive code conditioned model has much higher performance than the no conditioning model since it can disambiguate what task it needs to execute. Surprisingly language conditioning performs significantly better than primitive code conditioning in training task performance. We conjecture this is due to language conditioning being a more structured representation of the task, providing a better loss manifold from which the model can learn a better perceptual motor representation of the task distribution. In addition the training tasks are sampling initial states from a continuous distribution meaning almost no two policy rollouts have the same initial state. So some amount of generalization needs to be had even in the seen initial state distribution to have high performance on the training tasks.

The language conditioned model is significantly better in test task performance over the primitive code conditioning since it can use the language embedding to infer the target task determined by the goal text description. The primitive code conditioning model has access to the language embedding only to determine the closest training primitive code to the target task description. It compares the text description embedding of all the training tasks to the test task text description embedding and chooses the training task primitive code corresponding to the smallest L2 distance. It’s interesting to observe that the primitive code conditioning does better than no conditioning on the test task because the closest primitive code can sometimes lead to a successful execution of the test task since it has some signal towards the correct task. One such example is that the test goal description task of ”a blue block on the right of a yellow block” is mapped to the primitive code of the training goal description task of ”a red block on the right of a yellow block”.

In Table 2 we also show the same trend in the models performing at double the interval of initial starting locations of the objects seen at training (unseen intial state distribution): the no conditioning policy performing the poorest on train and test tasks, the primitive code conditioned model performing significantly better on both, the language conditioned model performing the best at training and test tasks via its language structured specification of tasks.

We train the multitask policy with ResNet18 image encoding that is concatenated with CLIP language encoding both of which are pushed through an fc layer before concatenation. The concatenated vector is then inserted into two fc layers before predicting actions. We apply an L2 regression loss for continuous action space. The policy is trained using Adam optimizer with AMS grad with learning rate of 1e-4. The images are augmented with PyTorch RandomResizedCrop of 0.95 to 1.0 area and 0.98 to 1.02 aspect ratio randomization and resized to original image dimensions of 128x128. All the multitask models are trained for 300 epochs.

## 4 RELATED WORK:

**Goal conditioned policies** Goal conditioned policies allow the user to specify the agent’s goal. States (Schaul et al., 2015; Andrychowicz et al., 2017) and Images (Pathak et al., 2018; Nair et al., 2018; Fu et al., 2018; Singh et al., 2019) are one way of specifying goal. However, they assume that user has access to a photo or state of final task given to the agent. We assume no access to goal images or state to minimize human labor and instrumentation, and use language which provides a natural form of supervision.

**Goal text conditioned policies** Several previous efforts train reward functions or policies that take natural language as input for goal description (Oh et al., 2017; Bahdanau et al., 2018; Zhou & Small, 2020; Goyal et al., 2020; Fu et al., 2019; Hermann et al., 2017; Shao et al., 2020). They all however rely on reward signals that have access to state of the system or demonstrations of the task distribution they are training on.

**Learning reward functions** There are works that use human videos to learn reward functions to train their agent with (Sermanet et al., 2018; 2016; Shao et al., 2020). We however, don’t need a curated dataset of humans performing the tasks we want our agent to train with. Having humans perform all tasks we are interested in may not scale well with the amount of labor needed for recording and curating those datasets. CLIP has the advantage of learning a caption model from 400 million image, text pairs from the publicly available sources on the internet WIT.

**No Environment Reward** There have been recent work on methods that use no reward signal from the environment to train for specific tasks. Two notable ones are Curiosity (Pathak et al., 2017) and DIAYN (Eysenbach et al., 2018). Curiosity has only been successful on video games with discrete action space and DIYAN has mostly been successful on direct state input. Our method however, can learn a subset of manipulation tasks in continuous action space on raw pixels and has the advantage of specification from user goal text.

**Utility of large vision language models in RL** There has been recent work that leveraged CLIP to do many complex manipulation tasks (Shridhar et al., 2021). They however, have access to labeled expert demonstrations for training their policy. We assume no access to demonstrations or goal images at training or test time.

## 5 DISCUSSION

In this work, we presented a method for learning a set of object manipulation tasks without access to state of the system to compute reward from text goal description alone. Our method doesn’t use goal images or demonstrations at training time or test time. We devised a zero-shot reward model that leverages a language vision model (CLIP) that has been trained on a very large dataset of captioned images on the internet to compute progress (reward) towards a goal text description. We use this zeroshot-reward model to collect data on many tasks to then supervise a language conditioned multi-task policy that can execute new tasks without need of extra training.

Our work opens avenues for many directions such as learning spatial relationships from data or leveraging separate datasets for spatial relationships. Pioneering work along these lines was proposed in (Guadarrama et al., 2013; Golland et al., 2010), who showed that spatial relationships could be grounded in a learning-based system. We encourage pursing the integration of these approaches to the grounding of spatial prepositions with the noun-phrase grounding capability we have demonstrated based on our CLIP-salience method.Our current method also does not specify state of the system other than semantic position such as object pose or closed door.

Finally we must address the ethical perils inherent in our leverage of models trained on large-scale vision and language datasets. Such datasets are well known to suffer from dataset bias that can cause failure or unintended harm (Buolamwini & Gebru, 2018). While the near-term risks appear to be limited with the robotic applications presently envisioned, practitioners should continuously monitor systems for bias against underrepresented groups and ensure that robotic systems work across all socioeconomic domains. Techniques for bias assessment and debiasing should be employed whenever possible to ensure this remains the case.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a compelling vision for zero-shot reward specification using grounded natural language, leveraging CLIP and saliency-based grounding to enable reward generation from raw pixels and text alone. It makes a strong case for reducing reliance on demonstrations or goal images, and the integration of spatial heuristics with language-driven object grounding is conceptually novel. The experimental setup on robotic manipulation tasks demonstrates practical progress toward language-conditioned policy generalization.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient validation of zero-shot generalization beyond color variation: The test-time generalization in Section 3.3 is limited to unseen object colors (blue block), while all spatial relationships and task types (stack, place-left, place-right) are present in training. True zero-shot generalization would require testing on novel compositions—e.g., 'blue block behind green block'—that were never seen during training. Without such compositional evaluation, the claim of zero-shot capability is overstated. The literature context explicitly raises compositional generalization as an open question, yet the paper does not address it. The authors should include a test set with novel spatial-object combinations to validate genuine zero-shot transfer.

- Overreliance on manually defined spatial heuristics without learning: The spatial grounding heuristics in Table 1 are entirely hand-coded and assume fixed camera views and coordinate semantics. This undermines the 'zero-shot' claim, as the system cannot generalize to new spatial relations (e.g., 'between and slightly closer to') without manual rule specification. The Discussion mentions learning spatial relationships as future work, but this is a core limitation: the method does not learn grounding but assumes it. To strengthen the contribution, the authors should either demonstrate learning of spatial rules from data or acknowledge that the approach is semi-automated and requires domain-specific engineering.

- Inadequate ablation of the grounding mechanism’s contribution: While Figure 6 shows the full model outperforming the base model, there is no ablation isolating the effect of Grad-CAM-based saliency versus alternative grounding methods (e.g., Mask R-CNN with fine-tuning). Figure 3 contrasts Grad-CAM with Mask R-CNN but only to argue the latter fails off-the-shelf—no quantitative comparison of reward quality or policy performance is provided. Without such ablation, it's unclear whether the saliency method is essential or merely one of several viable options. A direct comparison with a fine-tuned detector-based baseline would strengthen the argument for CLIP-saliency as a superior grounding mechanism.

- Unrealistic assumption of unambiguous language descriptions: The method assumes that natural language inputs like 'yellow block on the right of red block' are unambiguous and directly translatable into spatial rules. In practice, such descriptions can be context-dependent or underspecified (e.g., 'on the right' relative to viewer or object frame?). The paper does not evaluate robustness to paraphrasing or ambiguity, despite this being a known limitation in the literature. This risks poor real-world applicability. The authors should test performance on paraphrased instructions or include a human evaluation of interpretation consistency to support the robustness claim.

- Evaluation metric mismatch between training and reporting: The policies are trained using the proposed zero-shot reward model, but performance in Table 2 is reported using oracle reward (state-based). This conflates the quality of the learned policy with the fidelity of the reward model. If the goal is to validate the reward specification method, then the zero-shot reward should also be used for evaluation. Otherwise, it's unclear whether poor test performance stems from reward mis-specification or policy learning failure. The authors should report performance using the zero-shot reward itself to isolate the reward model’s effectiveness.

- Neglected scalability and computational cost of saliency maps: Generating Grad-CAM saliency maps for every object noun phrase at each timestep is computationally expensive, especially for multi-object tasks. The paper does not discuss inference latency or scalability to high-dimensional action spaces or complex scenes. Given that real-time robotic control often requires sub-100ms policy execution, the current approach may not be deployable. The authors should either provide timing benchmarks or acknowledge this as a critical limitation for practical use.

- No test of compositional generalization to novel spatial-object pairs: The paper claims zero-shot generalization but only evaluates on tasks that reuse training spatial relations (e.g., 'on the right of') with a single new color. A true test of zero-shot capability would require combinations absent from training—such as 'blue block behind green block'—where both the object and the spatial configuration are novel. Without this, the method may rely on memorized templates rather than compositional understanding. To validate the claim, the authors should define a held-out set of instruction-task pairs that compose known elements in new ways and report performance on them, following standard benchmarks in language-conditioned RL like ALFRED or BabyAI.

- Missing ablation comparing saliency-based grounding to learned alternatives: The paper presents Grad-CAM with CLIP as the sole grounding mechanism but provides no comparison to learnable alternatives, such as training a lightweight adapter on top of CLIP or using contrastive learning to align phrases with object locations. This omission leaves open whether the approach is truly zero-shot or merely shifts the burden to manual engineering of saliency rules. A minimal ablation would involve replacing the saliency module with a small trainable network that maps CLIP features to object coordinates, trained on a few labeled examples. Showing competitive performance would strengthen the zero-shot claim; failing to do so would highlight a dependency on handcrafted components.

- No analysis of reward model fidelity via human evaluation: The reward model is central to the method, yet its correctness is never assessed directly. The paper uses oracle reward for evaluation, which measures policy success but not whether the zero-shot reward accurately reflects the intended task. Readers will wonder whether the agent succeeds because the reward is good, or despite its flaws. To establish reward quality, the authors should sample image-instruction pairs and ask humans to rate whether the computed reward signal aligns with task progress—e.g., 'Does this frame look closer to achieving 'yellow block on red block' than the previous one?'—a standard practice in reward modeling.

- Unverified assumption that language-conditioned policy generalizes to new instructions: The multi-task policy is trained on 18 tasks and tested on 18 others, but all test instructions follow the same syntactic templates as training (e.g., '[color] block [spatial relation] [color] block'). The claim of generalization to 'unseen tasks' is therefore weak. A stronger test would use instructions with new syntax or semantics—e.g., 'put the blue block between the red and yellow blocks'—which requires understanding of multi-object spatial logic. The authors should either include such examples in evaluation or clarify that generalization is limited to lexical substitutions within fixed templates, not full compositional language understanding.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Include a compositional generalization test set with novel object-spatial combinations not seen during training to properly validate zero-shot claims

- Provide a quantitative ablation comparing the CLIP-saliency grounding against a fine-tuned object detector baseline in terms of reward quality and policy performance

- Evaluate robustness to language variation by testing on paraphrased or semantically equivalent instructions

- Report policy performance using the zero-shot reward model itself, not just oracle reward, to assess reward fidelity independently of policy learning

- Address computational feasibility by reporting inference times for saliency map generation or proposing a more efficient alternative

comments.title: Evaluation metric mismatch undermines reward model validation
comments.quote: While the policies were trained using our zero-shot reward function, the reward metric reported is oracle reward
comments.explanation: The use of oracle reward for evaluation—despite training with the proposed zero-shot reward—makes it impossible to assess the fidelity of the reward model itself. High performance under oracle evaluation could reflect a well-learned policy that compensates for a flawed reward signal, rather than accurate reward specification. If the goal is to validate zero-shot reward specification, then the same reward model used during training must be used at test time. Otherwise, the core contribution is not being measured. The authors should report episode returns using the zero-shot reward during rollout to isolate its effectiveness. This is especially important given that the oracle has privileged access to state, while the zero-shot model operates only on pixels and language.

comments.title: Ambiguous spatial coordinate definitions risk misalignment
comments.quote: The first set (left of, right of, on top of, below, and in between) assume coordinates in a front camera view and the semantics are defined in that camera view with positive y in the upward direction and positive x in the right direction. The second set (in front of & behind) has access to a left camera view with coordinate x2 pointing towards the right
comments.explanation: The description of the x2-axis is confusing: it is said to point 'towards the right' in the left camera view, which is also 'towards the front in the first camera view'. This blurs the distinction between lateral and depth axes. In a standard stereo setup, the left camera’s horizontal axis (x2) would correspond to a combination of the front view’s x and z (depth), not purely depth. As written, the definition risks misaligning the coordinate logic with actual camera geometry. For instance, the condition $O^1_{x2} > O^2_{x2}$ for 'in front of' assumes x2 encodes depth, but calling it 'right' suggests lateral motion. The authors should clarify the extrinsic calibration between cameras and define how pixel coordinates map to spatial relationships. A diagram or formal transformation would eliminate ambiguity.

comments.title: Dense evaluation reward conflates duration with success
comments.quote: Every timestep the objects are in the correct target text description the agent is rewarded 1 point. The objects never start in the correct target state. Therefore it always takes several timesteps for the policy to move the objects to the correct state. All episodes timeout after 100 timesteps or if the one of the objects falls off the table.
comments.explanation: Awarding 1 point per timestep in the goal state creates a dense reward during evaluation, which differs from the sparse oracle reward described in training (e.g., 'outputs one when [condition] is met'). This discrepancy inflates scores for policies that linger in the goal state, even if they are slow or inefficient. A fast, precise policy that achieves the goal at step 90 and holds it for 10 steps scores the same as one that reaches it at step 50 and idles. Since the training signal is sparse, the evaluation should reflect task completion—not sustained presence. The authors must either align evaluation with training by using a single reward upon achievement or justify why prolonged occupancy is part of the task objective. Otherwise, reported metrics do not reflect true task performance.

comments.title: Overstated claim of superior base model performance
comments.quote: Figure 4: We showcase the Base Reward Model in the Double Inverted Pendulum doing slightly better than oracle reward. We average results over 3 seeds per task.
comments.explanation: The claim that the base reward model 'does slightly better than oracle reward' is not supported by statistical evidence. Figure 4 shows overlapping confidence intervals, suggesting the difference may not be significant. Moreover, the oracle reward includes penalties for distance and velocity—components absent in the base model. The base model’s higher return could stem from avoiding these penalties rather than better task learning. Without ablation or significance testing, the comparison is misleading. The authors should rephrase to say performance is 'comparable' or provide statistical validation. Otherwise, readers may incorrectly infer that a simpler dot-product reward outperforms a carefully designed oracle, which weakens confidence in the analysis.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper presents a method for zero-shot reward specification in reinforcement learning using grounded natural language. The approach combines CLIP for object grounding with spatial relationship heuristics to generate rewards, then distills learned policies into a language-conditioned multi-task policy. While the core idea is promising and addresses an important challenge in RL, there are significant concerns about the robustness of the spatial grounding, the limited evaluation of zero-shot capabilities, and potential reward hacking issues.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by demonstrating how large vision-language models can be leveraged for reward specification without task-specific training. The factored approach separating 'what' from 'where/how' is clever, and the use of CLIP for zero-shot object grounding is well-motivated. However, the evaluation appears limited in scope, the spatial heuristics seem brittle, and the paper doesn't adequately address potential failure modes or safety concerns.

Overview issues:

- Brittle Spatial Grounding Heuristics: The spatial relationship rules in Table 1 are hardcoded heuristics that rely on precise object coordinates from 2D camera views. This approach has several weaknesses: (1) It assumes objects are always visible from specific camera angles, which fails with occlusions; (2) The epsilon thresholds for relationships like 'close to' and 'inside of' are arbitrary and likely don't transfer across environments; (3) The rules don't handle 3D spatial reasoning properly, using only 2D projections. The paper doesn't evaluate how sensitive performance is to these thresholds or how the system degrades with partial visibility. This undermines the claim of robust natural language grounding since spatial relationships are a core part of language instructions.

- Limited Zero-Shot Evaluation: The paper claims zero-shot reward specification but the evaluation is insufficient to support this claim. The multi-task policy is trained on 18 tasks and tested on 18 'unseen' tasks that only differ by object color (blue instead of red/green/yellow). This is not a meaningful test of zero-shot generalization - it's essentially testing color generalization rather than truly novel tasks or environments. There's no evaluation on completely new task types, different object arrangements, or new environments. The paper should include tests with genuinely novel spatial relationships or object combinations to demonstrate true zero-shot capability.

- Unaddressed Reward Hacking Vulnerabilities: The reward design is vulnerable to reward hacking behaviors that the paper doesn't analyze. Since the reward is 1 whenever spatial criteria are met, an agent could learn to exploit edge cases in the heuristics - for example, hovering at the exact threshold distance for 'close to' or finding camera angles that satisfy 'on top of' without actual stacking. The Grad-CAM based object localization could also be fooled by visually similar objects or background patterns. The paper lacks any analysis of failure modes or adversarial examples, which is crucial for a system that will autonomously generate rewards without human oversight.

- Inadequate Baseline Comparisons: The paper compares against weak baselines that don't properly contextualize the contribution. For the zero-shot reward model, it only compares against a naive dot-product baseline and curiosity-driven exploration, but not against more relevant approaches like language-conditioned inverse RL methods or recent VLM-based reward methods. For the multi-task policy, the primitive code conditioning baseline is artificial - why not compare against proper multi-task RL baselines or meta-learning approaches? The lack of meaningful comparisons makes it difficult to assess whether the performance gains come from the proposed method or simply from using CLIP features.

- Missing Analysis of Language Ambiguity: The paper assumes natural language instructions can be unambiguously parsed into object phrases and spatial relationships, but doesn't address how the system handles ambiguous or underspecified instructions. What happens with vague terms like 'near' vs 'close to', or with complex instructions involving multiple clauses? The parser for extracting object noun phrases and relationships is not described in detail. Given that ambiguity is a major challenge in language grounding, the paper should include experiments showing how performance degrades with increasingly ambiguous instructions.

- Missing concrete worked example: The paper would benefit from a complete worked example showing how a natural language instruction is transformed into rewards throughout training. For instance, trace the instruction 'place the blue block to the left of the red block' through: (1) parsing into object phrases and spatial relations, (2) CLIP encoding and Grad-CAM heatmap generation on specific observation frames, (3) coordinate extraction and spatial heuristic evaluation, and (4) resulting reward values at key timesteps. This would help readers understand the end-to-end process and verify that the components work together as claimed. The current figures show isolated pieces but not the complete pipeline in action.

- No demonstration of failure cases: The paper lacks systematic analysis of when and why the approach fails. While Figure 5 shows one failure of the base model, there is no comparable analysis for the full model. The paper should include examples where: (1) Grad-CAM fails to locate objects correctly, (2) spatial heuristics give incorrect rewards due to occlusion or camera angle, (3) the multi-task policy fails on truly novel task types. These failure cases would help readers understand the method's limitations and whether the claimed zero-shot capability is robust. For a method that automates reward specification, understanding failure modes is essential.

- Missing comparison to language-conditioned baselines: The paper positions itself against prior work but never formally compares with language-conditioned approaches. It claims advantages over methods that use state or demonstrations, but doesn't verify that those methods actually perform worse when given the same inputs. The paper should include experiments comparing against at least one recent language-conditioned RL method (e.g., Fu et al. 2019 or Zhou & Small 2020) adapted to use only pixels. Without this comparison, readers cannot assess whether the performance gains come from the proposed reward design or simply from using CLIP features.

- No guidance on hyperparameter selection: The spatial heuristics in Table 1 contain epsilon thresholds that are critical for performance but the paper provides no guidance on setting them. The values of epsilon_1, epsilon_2, and epsilon for 'close to' and 'inside of' relationships will significantly affect learning. The paper should include: (1) sensitivity analysis showing how performance varies with these thresholds, (2) discussion of how to choose them for new environments, and (3) whether they can be learned or adapted automatically. Without this guidance, practitioners cannot apply the method to new domains, undermining the claim of an off-the-shelf solution.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add comprehensive zero-shot evaluation with genuinely novel tasks and environments, not just color variations

- Analyze and address the brittleness of spatial heuristics, including sensitivity analysis and failure cases

- Include stronger baselines that properly contextualize the contribution, particularly recent VLM-based reward methods

- Provide detailed analysis of reward hacking vulnerabilities and potential safety concerns

- Evaluate performance on ambiguous or complex language instructions to understand the limits of the approach

comments.title: Undefined epsilon parameters in spatial heuristics
comments.quote:
Table 1: Spatial Grounding Heuristics

|  Spatial Relationship | Reward Criteria  |
| --- | --- |
|  Obj1 on the left of Obj2 | $O^2_x > O^1_x$ |
|  Obj1 on the right of Obj2 | $O^1_x > O^2_x$ |
|  Obj1 on top of Obj2 | $|O^1_x - O^2_x| < \epsilon_1 \& O^2_y < O^1_y < O^2_y + \epsilon_2$ |
|  Obj1 below Obj2 | $|O^1_x - O^2_x| < \epsilon_1 \& O^1_y < O^2_y < O^1_y + \epsilon_2$ |
|  Obj1 in between Obj2, Obj3 | $min(O^2_x, O^3_x) < O^1_x < max(O^2_x, O^3_x)$ |
|  Obj1 in front of Obj2 | $O^1_{x2} > O^2_{x2}$ |
|  Obj1 behind Obj2 | $O^2_{x2} > O^1_{x2}$ |
|  Obj1 close to Obj2 | $||O^1_{xy} - O^2_{xy}||_2 < \epsilon$ |
|  Obj1 inside of Obj2 | $||O^1_{xy} - O^2_{xy}||_2 < \epsilon$ |
comments.explanation: The spatial heuristics reference epsilon parameters ($\epsilon_1$, $\epsilon_2$, and $\epsilon$) but these values are never defined in the paper. The text mentions that "The $\epsilon$ threshold for 'inside of' is much smaller than for 'close to'" but provides no actual values or guidance on how to set them. This is a critical omission because these thresholds directly determine when spatial relationships are considered satisfied, affecting reward computation and learning. Without defined values, the method is not reproducible. Add explicit definitions for all epsilon parameters, including their numerical values used in experiments and guidance on how they were chosen.

comments.title: Inconsistent coordinate system in spatial heuristics
comments.quote: The first set (left of, right of, on top of, below, and in between) assume coordinates in a front camera view and the semantics are defined in that camera view with positive y in the upward direction and positive x in the right direction. The second set (in front of & behind) has access to a left camera view with coordinate x2 pointing towards the right
comments.explanation: There's an inconsistency in the coordinate system description. The text states that for the left camera view, x2 points "towards the right which is towards the front in the first camera view." This creates a contradiction - if x2 points towards the right in the left camera view, but this direction corresponds to "front" in the front camera view, then the coordinate systems are not aligned as claimed. This matters because the spatial heuristics in Table 1 rely on consistent coordinate comparisons across camera views. The description should clarify whether the coordinate systems are transformed to a common reference frame or if the heuristics account for these coordinate differences. Rewrite the coordinate description to explicitly state how the different camera coordinate systems are aligned or transformed before applying the spatial heuristics.

comments.title: Logical contradiction in multi-task evaluation setup
comments.quote:
In the FetchSimRobot environment we train 18 tasks with PPO each for 200K environ-

ment steps with our zero-shot reward model described in the previous section and show generalization results for 18 unseen test tasks by training a language conditioned policy with behavior cloning on rollouts of the 18 training tasks. We collect 5000 timesteps per task which is around 50 trajectories. We do this for pickplace-left, pickplace-right, and stack tasks for different object combinations. The object training colors are red, green, and yellow, and the object test color
comments.explanation: There's a logical inconsistency in the evaluation setup. The paper claims "zero-shot" capability but the multi-task policy is explicitly trained on rollouts from the 18 training tasks before being tested on the 18 unseen tasks. This is not zero-shot learning - it's transfer learning where the policy has seen training data. The zero-shot claim only applies to the initial reward function, not the final multi-task policy. The paper should clarify that the multi-task policy achieves few-shot generalization (after being trained on 18 tasks) rather than zero-shot generalization. This distinction matters because it affects how readers interpret the generalization capabilities.

comments.title: Ambiguous reward metric in multi-task evaluation
comments.quote: We use the same oracle reward that has direct access to state in figure 6 to measure the performance of our multi-task models. Every timestep the objects are in the correct target text description the agent is rewarded 1 point.
comments.explanation: The description of the reward metric is ambiguous. It states that the agent gets "1 point" when objects are in the correct configuration, but doesn't specify whether this is a dense reward (every timestep) or sparse reward (only at episode completion). Earlier in Section 3.2, the oracle reward was described as sparse: "outputs one when the centroid distance between two pucks are below a threshold." This inconsistency makes it unclear what metric is actually being reported in Table 2. Clarify whether the multi-task evaluation uses dense or sparse oracle rewards, and ensure consistency with the reward definitions used in the single-task experiments.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a zero-shot reward function for reinforcement learning by combining CLIP-based saliency maps for object grounding with rule-based spatial heuristics. It demonstrates that this approach allows for training robotic manipulation policies without state access or demonstrations, and evaluates a distilled language-conditioned policy on unseen tasks.

The paper introduces a clever, practical method for bridging the gap between natural language task descriptions and pixel-based reinforcement learning. By factoring the problem into object grounding and spatial logic, it avoids the common pitfalls of direct CLIP-embedding rewards. The results on robotic manipulation tasks provide convincing evidence for the effectiveness of this modular approach.

Overview issues:

- Sensitivity to Spatial Heuristic Assumptions: The proposed method relies on hard-coded spatial heuristics (Table 1) that assume specific camera perspectives and coordinate systems. For instance, the 'on top of' and 'behind' relationships require precise knowledge of camera orientation and tilt, which may not be available in general deployment settings. The paper does not address how the system would perform if the camera view deviates from these fixed configurations or if the environment contains occlusions that interfere with the saliency-based centroid calculation. To strengthen the work, the authors should include an ablation or sensitivity analysis demonstrating the system's performance under varying camera angles or noisy coordinate estimates.

- Lack of Robustness to Linguistic Ambiguity: The method parses natural language into noun phrases and spatial relationships, but the paper provides little detail on how it handles linguistic ambiguity or complex sentence structures. If a user provides a command like 'place the block near the red one' versus 'place the block to the right of the red one', the system's reliance on fixed parsing rules might fail to generalize. Since the CLIP-saliency approach is used to identify objects, it remains unclear how the system resolves references when multiple objects of the same class are present or when the description is non-standard. A discussion or experiment regarding the limits of the natural language parser would significantly improve the clarity of the 'zero-shot' claim.

- Reward Hacking and Sparse Signal Limitations: The reward function is essentially a binary check based on spatial heuristics, which can lead to sparse reward problems in complex tasks. While the authors use PPO, they do not discuss whether the agent experiences high variance or stagnation during training due to the lack of a dense shaping signal. Furthermore, the paper ignores the potential for reward hacking where the agent might satisfy the spatial heuristic (e.g., coordinates overlapping) without actually completing the intended manipulation (e.g., grasping or stacking). Including a qualitative analysis of the learned reward landscape or failure modes would help determine if the agent is truly learning the task or just exploiting the spatial coordinate thresholds.

- Generalization and Distribution Shift: The multi-task policy is evaluated on unseen object colors, but it is unclear if the model generalizes to unseen object shapes, sizes, or novel spatial configurations. The reliance on CLIP's zero-shot capabilities is a strength, but the policy distillation step uses behavior cloning on a limited set of training tasks. The paper should explicitly clarify if the policy's failure modes are due to the reward function's inaccuracies or the limitations of the distilled multi-task policy itself. Providing a more thorough analysis of the policy's performance under distribution shifts—beyond just color changes—would better justify the 'generalization' claims made in the introduction.

- Absence of failure-mode analysis for spatial heuristics: The paper relies on a set of hard-coded spatial heuristics (Table 1) to define reward, but it lacks a systematic analysis of when these heuristics fail. Specifically, the paper does not demonstrate how the system behaves when the Grad-CAM saliency maps produce noisy or multi-modal object center estimates, which are common in real-world visual scenes. To address this, the authors should provide a controlled experiment or simulation showing the agent's performance degradation as a function of increasing noise in the centroid estimation, or when the camera view deviates from the specific 45-degree tilt required by the 'inside of' heuristic. This would clarify the operational envelope of the proposed reward model.

- Lack of comparison to non-language-based reward shaping: The paper claims to provide a 'zero-shot' reward signal, yet it compares primarily against oracle state-based rewards and curiosity-driven baselines. It does not compare against a standard non-language, image-based reward shaping baseline, such as simple distance-to-goal-image metrics (e.g., L2 distance in latent space). Including a comparison against a standard goal-conditioned latent distance baseline would demonstrate whether the language-grounded reward actually provides superior guidance or if the performance gains are simply due to the specific architecture of the VLM. This is necessary to justify the claim that natural language is a superior communication interface for reward specification.

- Missing characterization of the reward landscape: The paper assumes that the proposed reward function provides a meaningful signal for PPO, but it does not visualize or analyze the reward landscape to ensure it is not overly sparse or deceptive. A critical gap is the lack of evidence that the reward function is robust to 'reward hacking,' where an agent might satisfy the spatial heuristic without correctly performing the manipulation (e.g., placing an object 'on top' by simply clipping through the target object). The authors should provide a qualitative analysis or a set of failure-case trajectories showing the agent's behavior when the reward signal is satisfied but the task is not completed, to verify the stability of the training process.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Include a sensitivity analysis of the spatial heuristics with respect to camera angle and coordinate noise to demonstrate robustness.

- Perform an ablation study or provide qualitative evidence regarding the policy's behavior when faced with linguistic ambiguity or multiple objects of the same class.

- Add a discussion or analysis of reward hacking potential and how the binary spatial rewards affect training stability in long-horizon tasks.

- Expand the evaluation to include at least one non-color-based generalization task (e.g., novel object shapes or sizes) to substantiate the generalization claims.

comments.title: Discrepancy between 'inside of' heuristic definition and stated purpose
comments.quote: For "inside of" a 45 degree camera helps the agent see if the object is going inside another object without occlusion. The $\epsilon$ threshold for "inside of" is much smaller than for "close to" since the centroid can come much closer when an object goes inside a container object.
comments.explanation: The paper justifies the use of a 45-degree camera view for the 'inside of' relationship by citing the need to handle occlusion. However, Table 1 defines the reward criteria for 'inside of' as simply the L2 norm of the pixel coordinates: ||O^1_xy - O^2_xy||_2 < epsilon. This definition is mathematically identical to the 'close to' relationship and lacks any logic to account for depth or occlusion. If the reward function relies solely on 2D centroid distance, the claim that the camera angle mitigates occlusion is unsupported. Please clarify if the reward function incorporates additional depth-dependent logic or if the current definition is insufficient for the stated goal.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a framework for zero-shot reward specification in robotic manipulation using CLIP and hand-coded spatial heuristics, followed by behavioral cloning to create a multi-task policy. The approach separates object grounding from spatial relationship evaluation to bypass the limitations of simple CLIP dot-product rewards. While the idea of factoring 'what' and 'where' is intuitive, the implementation relies heavily on coordinate-based heuristics that conflict with the stated zero-shot, pixel-only constraints.

The paper tackles an important problem in reward specification for embodied AI. The separation of phrase grounding and spatial reasoning is a logical step forward from naive CLIP-based rewards. The experimental setup on standard simulation environments provides a clear baseline for comparison. However, the gap between the theoretical claims of state-free zero-shot learning and the practical reliance on coordinate-based heuristics weakens the contribution.

Overview issues:

- Privileged State Information Contradicts Zero-Shot Claims: The introduction explicitly states the agent learns purely from pixels with no access to the underlying state of the environment. The method section immediately undermines this by computing rewards based on object coordinates derived from Grad-CAM heatmaps. While the authors distinguish these as pixel coordinates, the spatial heuristics in Table 1 treat them as direct proxies for metric state. This conflates pixel-space localization with environment state, effectively leaking privileged information into the reward signal. Readers will question how this approach generalizes to real-world cameras where pixel-to-metric mapping is non-trivial. The authors should clarify whether these heuristics operate in pixel space or require a calibrated metric transformation, and adjust the claims to reflect the actual reliance on coordinate information.

- Rigid Spatial Heuristics Lack Generalization Analysis: Table 1 defines spatial relationships using fixed thresholds and strict coordinate inequalities. These rules are highly sensitive to camera distance, object scale, and viewpoint. The paper provides no ablation study on how performance degrades when these thresholds are perturbed, nor does it discuss how the heuristics adapt to different environments or camera setups. This rigidity contradicts the off-the-shelf promise. A sensitivity analysis or a discussion on how these parameters are transferred across domains is necessary to establish the method's practical utility.

- Severe Performance Drop on Unseen Object Colors: Table 2 reports a dramatic decline in performance when testing on blue objects compared to the red, green, and yellow objects used during training. The language-conditioned policy drops from a training mean of 43.62 to a test mean of 14.79. The authors attribute this partly to the unseen initial state distribution, but the color shift is a significant domain change that the behavioral cloning policy fails to handle. Since the core contribution is zero-shot generalization, this drop is critical. The paper needs a deeper error analysis to determine whether the failure stems from the BC policy's inability to generalize visual features, limitations in CLIP's language conditioning, or issues with the reward shaping itself.

- Flawed Comparison to Oracle Reward: The experimental results compare the proposed reward model against an oracle reward that uses true x,y,z positions. These two reward functions measure fundamentally different quantities: one relies on noisy, pixel-based centroid localization, while the other uses ground-truth state. Claiming the proposed model performs almost as well as oracle sparse reward is misleading because the reward landscapes are not aligned. A more appropriate comparison would involve standard dense reward shaping baselines or other vision-language reward models to demonstrate that the proposed factored approach actually provides a learning advantage over simpler alternatives.

- Missing Analysis of Grad-CAM Limitations in Robotics: The paper relies on Grad-CAM to extract object centroids from CLIP's image encoder. While Figure 3 shows promising results, Grad-CAM is known to suffer from localization inaccuracies, especially with occlusions, transparent objects, or complex backgrounds common in robotics. The paper does not discuss how the reward signal degrades when Grad-CAM fails to pinpoint the correct object, nor does it address how the RL agent reacts to noisy or drifting centroids. Including a failure case analysis or a robustness test under occlusion would clarify the practical limits of this grounding strategy.

- Undefined epsilon thresholds for spatial reward rules: Table 1 defines spatial relationships using fixed inequalities with parameters epsilon_1, epsilon_2, and epsilon, but the manuscript never reports their numerical values. Without these constants, readers cannot reproduce the reward signal or understand the tolerance for spatial placement. The text vaguely notes that the inside of threshold is smaller than for close to, but this qualitative description leaves the actual sensitivity of the reward function unknown. Providing the exact hyperparameter values is essential for reproducibility and allows practitioners to tune the rules for different camera resolutions or object scales. The authors should append a table or appendix listing the precise epsilon values used across all environments.

- No error analysis for the unseen color generalization drop: Table 2 shows a severe performance collapse when testing on blue objects, dropping from a training mean of 43.62 to a test mean of 14.79. The authors attribute this partly to the initial state distribution but do not isolate whether the failure stems from the behavioral cloning policy, the CLIP language conditioning, or the reward shaping itself. A concrete error analysis is necessary to determine if the policy simply memorizes training colors or if the reward signal becomes uninformative for unseen hues. The paper would benefit from visualizing failure trajectories or running an ablation where the BC policy is tested on training colors but with the test initial state distribution. This would clarify whether the bottleneck lies in visual feature extraction or policy generalization.

- Absent comparison to other vision-language reward models: The experimental section compares the proposed reward against oracle rewards and curiosity-driven exploration, but it omits direct comparisons to other vision-language reward learning methods. Prior work like Pix2Reward (Goyal et al., 2020) or CLIP-based reward models also leverage large vision-language models to generate dense rewards from text. Without benchmarking against these approaches, the paper cannot substantiate its claim that the factored grounding strategy provides a distinct learning advantage. The authors should add a baseline using a direct CLIP dot-product reward on the same manipulation tasks or compare against a standard dense reward shaping method. This comparison would demonstrate whether the spatial heuristics actually improve sample efficiency or final performance over simpler grounding techniques.

- Missing analysis of spatial rule sensitivity to viewpoint changes: The spatial heuristics rely on strict pixel-coordinate inequalities that assume a fixed camera pose and scale. The paper does not evaluate how these rules degrade when the camera zooms, rotates, or when objects undergo significant rotation. A worked example computing the reward for a single task under varying camera distances would reveal whether the heuristic thresholds hold or require recalibration. Readers will wonder how this approach translates to real-world deployments where camera calibration drifts. Including a sensitivity analysis or a discussion on how to normalize pixel coordinates to a canonical frame would address this practical limitation.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper presents a conceptually clear method for factoring visual grounding and spatial reasoning, but the implementation relies on coordinate-based heuristics that contradict the stated zero-shot, state-free claims. The severe performance drop on unseen object colors and the flawed oracle comparison weaken the experimental validation. Addressing these issues requires clarifying the state information leakage, providing sensitivity analyses for the spatial thresholds, and conducting a deeper error analysis on the multi-task policy's generalization capabilities.

Revision targets:

- Clarify the distinction between pixel coordinates and environment state, and adjust the theoretical claims to accurately reflect the reliance on coordinate-based reward shaping.

- Provide a sensitivity analysis for the spatial heuristics thresholds to demonstrate how robust the reward function is to camera distance and object scale variations.

- Conduct a detailed error analysis on the multi-task policy's performance drop when switching to blue objects, isolating whether the failure originates from the BC policy, CLIP conditioning, or reward noise.

- Replace or supplement the oracle reward comparison with baselines that use comparable information sources, such as standard dense reward shaping or other vision-language reward models.

- Include a failure case analysis demonstrating how the reward signal behaves under Grad-CAM localization errors, occlusions, or viewpoint changes.
